title: How to Use a Bread Lame for Cross-Hatch Sourdough Patterns
meta_description: Master cross hatch sourdough scoring with step‑by‑step tips and the 3‑in‑1 Sourdough Whisk Set. Learn how to use a bread lame for perfect patterns every time.
html:
Scoring a loaf isn’t just about letting steam escape – it’s also the canvas for your bakery‑style art. When you aim for a cross hatch sourdough scoring pattern, the right technique and tools make all the difference. Below you’ll find a practical workflow, from dough preparation to the final slash, plus a look at the 3‑in‑1 Sourdough Whisk Set (whisk, scraper, and bread scoring lame) that can simplify the whole process for beginners.

Why the Cross Hatch Pattern Works

The intersecting lines of a cross hatch create a controlled expansion point that encourages the loaf to rise evenly while showcasing a clean, professional look. The pattern also helps distribute steam across the surface, reducing uneven bubbling and giving the crust a uniform golden‑brown finish.

Preparing the Dough for Scoring

Before you even pick up a lame, the dough must be set up for a crisp cut:

  1. Bulk Ferment – Allow the starter to reach its peak activity (usually 4–6 hours at 78°F). A well‑fermented dough will have a light, airy crumb that responds well to scoring.
  2. Shape the Loaf – After the first fold, shape the dough into a tight boule or batard. Tight surface tension ensures the lines you cut won’t collapse.
  3. Final Proof – Place the shaped loaf in a banneton, cover, and let it proof until it passes the “poke test.” A slightly under‑proofed loaf holds its shape better during the cut.

Once the loaf is ready, gently dust the surface with flour to reduce sticking, then preheat your oven with a Dutch oven or a baking stone inside.

Choosing the Right Lame

The bread scoring lame is essentially a razor blade mounted on a handle. For a clean cross hatch, you need a blade that is sharp, thin, and easy to control. The Sourdough Whisk Set includes a stainless‑steel lame that is 2 inches long, perfect for both beginners and seasoned bakers. Its ergonomic handle gives you the leverage to make deep, consistent cuts without wobbling.

Step‑by‑Step Cross Hatch Scoring

Follow these steps to achieve a balanced cross hatch:

  • 1. Position the loaf on a parchment‑lined peel or a floured board. The top should be facing up and centered.
  • 2. Angle the lame at about 30–45° to the dough surface. A shallow angle yields a shallow cut; a steeper angle gives a deeper slash.
  • 3. Make the first line – Drag the lame quickly across the center of the loaf, aiming for a length that covers roughly two‑thirds of the diameter.
  • 4. Rotate the loaf 90° and repeat the same motion, intersecting the first line at the midpoint. The two lines form the classic cross.
  • 5. Add depth – If you want a more dramatic effect, make a second set of shorter lines parallel to the first two, about ¼‑inch away on each side.
  • 6. Transfer to the oven – Slide the scored loaf onto the preheated stone or into the Dutch oven, then bake according to your recipe.

Practice makes perfect: experiment with line length and angle until you find the look that matches your style.

Using the 3‑in‑1 Sourdough Whisk Set to Streamline the Process

Beyond the lame, the Sourdough Whisk Set offers two additional tools that can improve your overall sourdough routine:

  • Danish Dough Whisk – Ideal for mixing starter and flour without over‑developing gluten. The whisk’s wire design creates a gentle, aerated dough, which contributes to a lighter crumb—perfect for a clean scoring surface.
  • Dough Scraper – Use the scraper to turn and fold the dough during bulk fermentation. A well‑folded dough has a tighter structure, making the cross hatch lines hold their shape better during baking.

Having all three tools in one kit eliminates the need to hunt for separate pieces, and the consistent quality of the lame ensures every slash is as sharp as the first.

Troubleshooting Common Scoring Issues

If your cross hatch pattern looks uneven or the dough collapses, consider these variables:

  • Blade dullness – A dull lame tears instead of cutting, resulting in ragged lines. Sharpen or replace the blade before each bake.
  • Proofing level – Over‑proofed dough is too slack, causing cuts to close quickly. Adjust timing or temperature to keep the dough firm.
  • Steam – Insufficient steam can dry the surface before the cut opens, leading to shallow cracks. Add a tray of hot water or spray the oven with water right after scoring.

By fine‑tuning these factors, you’ll see a crisp, clean cross hatch sourdough scoring pattern that blooms beautifully in the oven.

FAQ

Do I need a special lame for a cross hatch pattern?

No special blade is required, but a thin, sharp blade like the one in the Sourdough Whisk Set provides the consistency needed for clean intersecting lines.

Can I use the same scoring technique for whole‑grain loaves?

Yes, but whole‑grain doughs are denser, so you may need to apply a bit more pressure and make slightly longer cuts to achieve the same visual impact.

How often should I replace the blade on my lame?

With regular use, a blade will stay sharp for 10–15 loaves. When you notice the cuts becoming ragged or the blade no longer gliding smoothly, it’s time to replace it.
